title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Olympic Games. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Sacred Flame: From Olympia to the Modern Era
content.text: The Olympic Games are the world’s most famous sporting event, but their origins are deeply rooted in the religious and political life of Ancient Greece. Starting in 776 BC, the games were held every four years in honor of Zeus, the king of the gods. They were more than just a competition; they were a sacred festival that brought together citizens from across the fragmented Greek world. ||(41)|| [41]This period of peace, known as the 'ekecheiria,' allowed athletes and spectators to travel safely to the sanctuary of Olympia, even if their respective city-states were at war[/41]. For five days, all hostilities were suspended, and the focus shifted to the pursuit of 'arete'—a Greek concept representing excellence, honor, and physical perfection. ||(42)|| [42]The early events were modest, beginning with a single footrace called the 'stade.' However, the program gradually expanded to include wrestling, boxing, chariot racing, and the pentathlon[/42]. Winners did not receive medals, but were crowned with a wreath of olive leaves and granted hero status in their home cities. ||(43)|| [43]This ancient tradition came to an abrupt end in 393 AD[/43]. The Roman Emperor Theodosius I, a devout Christian, viewed the games as a pagan festival and ordered them to be abolished as part of his campaign to establish Christianity as the state religion. The site of Olympia fell into ruin, buried by earthquakes and floods for over a millennium. ||(44)|| [44]Inspired by the archaeological rediscovery of the site, a French educator named Baron Pierre de Coubertin proposed a revival of the games[/44]. He believed that international sporting competition could foster peace and understanding between nations, serving as a modern version of the ancient Greek truce. ||(45)|| [45]The first modern games were held in Athens in 1896, featuring 241 athletes from 14 nations[/45]. While small by today’s standards, the event was a resounding success, capturing the public imagination and proving that the Olympic spirit was still alive. ||(46)|| [46]As we look toward future games, the Olympics face modern challenges like commercialization and doping[/46]. Yet, the core symbol of the event—the relay of the sacred flame—remains a powerful reminder of our shared human heritage. The games continue to be a stage where the world comes together, if only for a few weeks, to celebrate the limits of human potential.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] a sacred truce was declared [/41] throughout the duration of the games. This was not merely a polite agreement, but a religiously enforced law that protected the sanctity of the festival from the chronic infighting of the Greek city-states.
paragraphs.B: This [42] dedication to excellence [/42] was often brutal. In events like the 'pankration,' a form of combat with almost no rules, athletes fought with a ferocity that often resulted in serious injury, all for the sake of bringing glory to their families and their gods.
paragraphs.C: Despite [43] the prestige associated with these victories [/43], the games were only open to free-born Greek men. Women, foreigners, and slaves were strictly excluded from competition, a reflection of the rigid social hierarchies that defined the ancient world.
paragraphs.D: The [44] silence of the stadium lasted for fifteen centuries [/44]. It wasn't until the late 19th century that interest in the classical world began to peak again, driven by a new generation of historians and physical educators who saw value in the ancient ideals.
paragraphs.E: His [45] vision for a global festival [/45] led to the formation of the International Olympic Committee (IOC). Coubertin argued that 'the important thing in life is not the triumph but the struggle,' a philosophy that still underpins the Olympic movement today.
paragraphs.F: This [46] gradual expansion into a global phenomenon [/46] saw the introduction of the Winter Games, the Paralympics, and the inclusion of female athletes. The Olympics transformed from an amateur gathering into a massive multi-billion dollar media event that stops the world every four years.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern critics argue [/47] that the focus on 'winning at all costs' has corrupted the original spirit of the games. They point to the professionalization of sports as a departure from the ancient ideal of the amateur athlete who competes purely for the sake of honor.

questions.explanation: A introduces the 'sacred truce', which directly explains the 'period of peace' and the ability of athletes to travel safely mentioned in the text.
B links the 'dedication to excellence' back to the concept of 'arete' (excellence/honor) and provides examples of the 'brutal' early events.
C refers to the 'prestige associated with these victories' (olive wreaths and hero status) and adds context about the social exclusions of the ancient games.
D bridges the fifteen-century gap between the abolition of the games by Theodosius and the 19th-century revival mentioned after the gap.
E explains Coubertin's 'vision for a global festival' and the formation of the IOC, following the mention of his proposal to revive the games.
F describes the 'gradual expansion' (Winter Games, Paralympics) that followed the initial success of the 1896 Athens games.
